Text: Ligotti frames his exquisitely morose series of essays on antinatalism with the provocative (and unabashedly materialist) assessment of man as a  self-conscious nothing,  a pessimistic assertion which most would reject out of hand as morbid nonsense, running as it does counter to the near-universally held sense of optimism about our nature and future, as promulgated through the hackneyed maxims and trite platitudes that are so often trotted out in daily life, usually in the form of a neat, smug paradox (see Chesterton) intended to safeguard the illusory tranquility of one s selfhood against the barrages of logic and circumstance; however, through the very desperation with which these tortured epigrams are constructed and pronounced, the optimist shows his hand, betraying his own self as the true paradox in question, a self that is not a self at all, but rather an uncanny puppet which broken free of its strings can be neither fully toy nor fully boy only some uncanny conjoining of the two, an abomination of nightmarish aspect whose existence can be only loosely conceived of in works of supernatural horror but never definitively demonstrated, and certainly never widely accepted as fact, since in so doing, we would be forced to encounter the face in the mirror and find ourselves hideously lacking in one sense and grotesquely endowed in the other.

CONSPIRACY AGAINST THE HUMAN RACE
Introduction: Of Pessimism and Paradox
Ligotti frames his exquisitely morose series of essays on antinatalism with the provocative (and unabashedly materialist) assessment of man as a  self-conscious nothing,  a pessimistic assertion which most would reject out of hand as morbid nonsense, running as it does counter to the near-universally held sense of optimism about our nature and future, as promulgated through the hackneyed maxims and trite platitudes that are so often trotted out in daily life, usually in the form of a neat, smug paradox (see Chesterton) intended to safeguard the illusory tranquility of one s selfhood against the barrages of logic and circumstance; however, through the very desperation with which these tortured epigrams are constructed and pronounced, the optimist shows his hand, betraying his own self as the true paradox in question, a self that is not a self at all, but rather an uncanny puppet which broken free of its strings can be neither fully toy nor fully boy only some uncanny conjoining of the two, an abomination of nightmarish aspect whose existence can be only loosely conceived of in works of supernatural horror but never definitively demonstrated, and certainly never widely accepted as fact, since in so doing, we would be forced to encounter the face in the mirror and find ourselves hideously lacking in one sense and grotesquely endowed in the other.
[Paradox lies at the heart of human nature. Consciousness first grants us a sense of (supernatural) self and then drives us toward the (natural) conclusion that this sense of self is illusory, rendering us split beings uncanny puppets. Optimists (the majority of humanity) attempt to subvert this paradox function (by way of epigram, platitude, maxim) toward legitimization of our special status in the universe, but the pessimistic tradition (rooted in Darwinian materialism) maintains that  man is a self conscious nothing" (Bahnsen), although definitive proof of either position remains elusive.]
The nightmare of being
The vast majority of people agree that  Being alive is alright.  However, there are those unfortunate few who, due to some combination of genetic inclination, temperamental idiosyncrasy, or morbid thought, disagree. We call them pessimists. 
Norwegian philosopher Zapffe believes that the pessimistic impulse originated from a surplus of consciousness that developed somewhere along the evolutionary line. The development of consciousness was, asserts Zapffe, a blunder on the part of nature, one that rendered man a split being, knowledgeable of its own demise but unable to avoid it.
To obscure this fact so that we might overcome our kenophobic/thanatophobic fear of the void and continue on living as a species, Zapffe posits that there are four methods we use to shield ourselves from the assailments of pessimism: 
      Isolation   relegation of dire facts of life to remote compartment of the mind
      Anchoring   belief in institutional verities such as God, Country, Morality, etc.
      Distraction   diversion through preoccupation with trifling or momentous trash
      Sublimation   acknowledgement and subversion (e.g. the heroic pessimist)
Michaelstader expanded on these methods in his work, asserting that we construct repressional illusions rooted in philopsychia [love of self] to hide ourselves from the terrible truth. Schopenhauer calls this philopsychic impulse the Will-to-Live, a blind force (anima mundi, elan, etc.) that compels us forward. Mainlander, a disciple of Schopenhauer, agrees that there is a blind force that but reimagines it as a Will-to-Die that proceeds from a Deicidal tendency toward divine self-annihilation that resides in the hearts and souls of all living things.
Of course, these pessimistic strains of thought cannot be carried out to their logical conclusions since they fundamentally contradict our biological imperatives hence, even the most dedicated pessimists find ways to sublimate and subvert their dark broodings. Philosophers like Unamuno, Camus, and others advocate a kind of  heroic pessimism  that persists in spite of (and because of) the suffering that we must endure as split beings
Others, like Lovecraft, find relief from their angst/boredom in the form of ontological ecstasy/terror, envisioning the weird within frames of reference that still maintain some semblance of normalcy (or even nostalgia, e.g. Lovecraft s fetishization of Novanglian domesticity/purity).
Still others, like Zappfe himself, throw themselves into causes (e.g. biosophy), advocating for expanded conceptions of ecological system that takes the suffering of nonhuman beings into account.
However, these coping mechanisms still fail to the address the ultimate issue at hand which is that human existence is (at least within the materialist, evolutionary worldview) an unmitigated evil, or, as Ligotti puts it, MALIGNANTLY USELESS. To bring new life into this world is, necessarily, to generate new suffering; to abstain from procreation is to prevent it.
Thus, accordingly to Zapffe, Benatar, and other antinatalist pessimists (such as Ligotti himself), it is incumbent upon us to stop reproducing, thereby take corrective action against nature s blundering in a kind of  negative utilitarianism  that demands both abstaining from any action that might generate pain, and taking any action that might alleviate it.  
When it comes to dealing with suffering, the repression of consciousness through mechanisms like isolation will work for most, giving them grounds for hope, however dim or tenuous; but if we are ever to eliminate once and for all the suffering that everyone must endure (optimist and pessimist alike) in our tireless striving for unattainable utopian futures, we must take our collective fate into our own hands and hasten the only future that truly can be attained oblivion. 
Is this likely to happen? No, the suppressive force of the optimistic majority is too great. What then? Is the pessimist not a hypocrite for continuing to live despite his assertion that life is not all right, that it is to the contrary a great evil? No, he is not, at least from a relativistic point of view.
That is to say, all actions taken within our nightmarish frame of existence necessarily reinforce it, just as using the potato masher to mash a potato (or not) reinforces its intended function in relation to its enacted function. Suicide is still an affirmation of life, albeit through negation. 
While absolutist systems of thought like Christianity and Islam chafe against this potato-mashing relativism, others such as Buddhism (or Lovecraft s indifferentism) simply cross their arms and accept the tensions inherent in our nightmarishly paradoxical nature. In either case, the mashing goes on, turning our gross materiality into pulp from which new abominations may grow.
Nothing short of extinction will do, says Ligotti.
The problem is and Ligotti acknowledges it that by advocating the antinalist viewpoint, he implicitly undermines it. We are torn creatures and everything we say or do pulls in two directions simultaneously.
Thus, it seems to me, the attempt to construct a pessimistic argument or really any argument at all, is ultimately futile. Ligotti speaks frequently of  logic  and scientific advancement as supporting the need for self-extinction of the species. But this line of thinking only works from a materialist standpoint wherein all assertions of the possibility of external realities beyond the empirically verifiable are dismissed as mere  repressional modes. 
The potato masher mashes on, true, but are there not other tools at our disposal? Could we not julienne the potato, or dice it, or perhaps discover some new way?
We may not be able to change the potato s nature, or the masher s, or our own, but let us not be so hubristic as to suppose we fully understand what those natures are or what new possibilities they may contain.
Ligotti is acutely aware, of course, of the ridiculous contradictions in writing a book about how no books ought to be written creating toward uncreation. 
But where he errs is in thinking that the only resolution to this tension is to mash it up. The science is not definitive, the suffering is not insuperable. Perhaps the uncanny is but an intermediate phase in our becoming something more, something whole, something real.
[In order to maintain the delusion that  being alive is alright  and thereby escape the nightmare of being, the optimist employs various evasive maneuvers, which Zapffe termed isolation (repressing the deeply known truth), anchoring (grounding the groundless in  institutional verities ), distraction (mindless consumption), and sublimation (conscious subversion), with the most prominent example of the latter being the  heroic pessimists  (e.g. Camus, Brashear, Unamuno, Dienstag etc.) who although they stand against the tired truisms of pop psychology and the positivity movement still implicitly prop up the nightmare world with exhortations to  persist despite it all.  Hardline pessimists (e.g. antinatalists) like Zappfe, Mainlander, Michelstader, Benatar and Ligotti, on the other hand, assert that nothing short of deliberate self-annihilation (the Will to Die [Schopenhauer reloaded]) will do as a corrective measure against the evolutionary  blunder  of consciousness, a malignantly useless  potato-masher  of existence (relative to Nothing).]
Who goes there?
In this chapter, Ligotti expounds upon the  uncanny wrongness  of the human in its mechanical aspect. When individuals cease to appear integrated in their identities (e.g. the epileptic fit, morbus sacer), the onlooker (or inlooker) is forced to encounter the  dark knowledge  of causal dependency and deterministic eventuality (i.e. man as the self-conscious automaton). In these moments, we see ourselves for what we are supernatural horrors who prey, auto-vampirically, upon the blood/brains of our own undead natures. Attempts to break down these  tightly packed layers of simulation  and exorcise the  I  through logic or reason are futile, however, since our ability to exercise logic and reason is predicated upon our unfounded, emotionally fundamental, and ultimately insuperable convictions/fabrications of selfhood and agency, a sense shared by optimist and pessimist alike. Science suggests that both philosophical proclivities the optimistic and the pessimistic can be reduced to psychological/physiological predisposition, nothing more (perhaps nothing at all). And this is Metzinger s paradox: you cannot know what you really are because then you would know there is nothing to know and no one to know it.
Freaks of salvation
Similar to Metzinger s quandary of knowing (to know is to know you cannot know) is the Buddhist paradox of wanting (to want is not to get). In other words, our desire for salvation as a species (from what, no one can say) is the very thing that blocks or obviates the achievement thereof. From this place of shared recognition/resignation re: the paradox at the heart of human nature, Ligotti outlines a presuppositional/soteriological congruence between the pessimistic tradition and Eastern thought, both asserting as they do that life is suffering and that this suffering can be overcome (albeit through varying means).
For the Buddhist, salvation/enlightenment comes by way of strict adherence to a set of meditative practice, spiritual disciplines, and doctrinal guidelines, whereas the hardline pessimist seeks to get beyond prescriptive guidelines altogether through repudiation/obliteration of the (Un)Real. There are, of course, different breeds or strands of pessimism the Nietzchean pessimism of strength (or materialistic mysticism, heroic perseverance) the life-negating pessimism of the Will-to-Live (a la Schopenhauer), or the perverse pessimism of Lovecraft (terror as ecstasis)--each with its own salvific mechanisms and implications. Regardless of the one tack adopts, however, the goal remains the same a rejection of the illusive desires, hopes, and dreams that plague our freakish natures.
Standing against the Buddhist-pessimist school are the transhumanists zealots who proselytize a new gospel of hyperconsciousness, envisioning utopian futures and states of existence that vastly exceed our own. Ligotti maintains that while such futures might be theoretically possible, they are without precedent, and thus inconceivable sans existing frames of reference which as discussed previously are tainted with the repressional rhetorics of optimism. As history has demonstrated time and again, any attempt to construct a unified and monolithic vision of human ascendancy (vis a vis Babel) be it through technology, reason, or the denouncement thereof has failed, definitively and colossally.
Going off the accumulated annals of human experience and medical science, the only remotely plausible means of transformative/salvific enlightenment is that of the isolated incident or freak accident, as in the anomalous cases of Krishnamurti, Wren-Lewis, and Segal, who through some traumatic incident (often a Near Death Eexperience) are miraculously bereft of ego and its attendant anxieties. The burden of consciousness was (at least according to the materialist account of things) bestowed upon us through dumb luck, and only an anomalous circumstance of a similar kind can relieve us from its hideous tormentations. 
Sick to death
As self-conscious unselves, aware of their fate and terrified to meet it, we employ a variety of coping mechanisms in order to overcome the thanatophoba that plagues us, ranging from the Frankensteinian (the scientist s mad, occult obsession with the generation and extension of life as the Great Good) xenophobic/genocidal (Terror Management Theory as offering posthumous prolongation of the self through identification with the greater body [social, familial, etc.], and denial/eradication of other bodies or framework that threaten to destabilize), the suicidal (Gloria in McCoy s They Shoot Horses, Don t They), the sublimational (recognition of coping mechanisms at work as a kind of meta-mechanism, exhibited by Zapffe and Tolstoy in their fourfold enumerations of repressional modes: ignorance/isolation, epicureanism/distraction, weakness/anchoring, strength/sublimation), and religious (Tolstoy s preferred method of evasion,  living by one s gut,  as recounted in A Confession, a pseudo-pessimistic cop out [ fa ade of ruin, ] per Zapffe). Ultimately, all of these repressional modes yes, even sublimation are rooted in the  propaganda of tomorrow,  purgative affirmations of life, the sickly sweet taste of which only supernatural horror (see Sondheim s Sweeney Todd) can wash from our mouths, satisfying (albeit only temporarily) our thirst for tragedy and our hunger for flesh,  the food that talks. 
The cult of grinning martyrs
As our knowledge of the processes at work within and upon us grows (specifically, the understanding of pleasure as an adaptive evolutionary mechanism that incentivizes actions tending toward survival), so too do the strategies by which we hide this knowledge from ourselves. In the absence of definitive proof either for or against existence, we have pressures, akin to bowel obstructions or movements (e.g. posterity, status, integration into the social unit by way of childbirth, the blood sacrifice). None of these pressures or incentives are praiseworthy   all stink of  existence bias,  which is to say we argue for existence from a state of existence that prejudices us in favor of it.
Pressure turns upon itself, however, as consciousness emerges, compelling us toward the total view of our function in the universe, namely, to know that we will die. In this sense, we are, as Ligotti puts it,  victims of the sepulchral pomp of rotting tissue,  a skeletal grin writ large across the face of an uncaring world.
Autopsy on a puppet: an anatomy of the supernatural
In this final chapter, Ligotti explores atmosphere as a matrix of horror emerging from consciousness with its suggestion of  an ominous state of affairs beyond the sensory.  
He proceeds to trace the evolution of atmosphere in supernatural horror fiction, beginning with Ann Radcliffe, whose novels such as Mysteries of Udolpho (1794) depict the terrorization of young women at the hands of the gargantuan, the macabre, the masculine forces from which they are ultimately rescued and whisked away to a happily ever after. With Poe s Fall of the House of Usher (1839), this picturesque, romanticized gothic world is replaced with a wholly evil atmosphere of death and doom. Century later, Lovecraft elevates this sense of horror to the cosmic level with  Call of Cthulu,  positioning not only the narrator, but all of humanity within the jaws of universal madness, extinction, and terror.
To further illustrate the pessimistic reverberations of atmosphere across literary instances (as well as public preference for instances in which  the game of good and evil  is played according to long-established rules), Ligotti contrasts Topor s The Tenant (1964)--a little-known novel wherein the protagonist is tormented by fellow tenants and driven to attempt suicide, only to discover that his was the body observed on the hospital bed earlier in the novel (i.e. this has happened before and will again)--against Pirandello s One, No One, and One Hundred Thousand (1926)--a Nobel Prize-winning novel that describes its protagonist s utter displacement of self upon recognizing his misperception about the placement of his nose on his face, a realization that forces him to dissolve his ego into the immediacy of the moment. Atmosphere hangs heavy over The Tenant, dooming it to obscurity, whereas Pirandello dissipates the mist so that life may go on, and his readership thanked him with a Nobel Prize.
Although not pessimistic in the purest sense, Conrad s Heart of Darkness (1902) exhibits many of these atmospheric tendencies previously described, pushing its narrator (and the reader) toward the locus of tragedy, the disquieting interior, that Kurtzean horror that lies at the center of it all.
Holding up Hamlet (the superficial tragedy of human errors) against Macbeth (the deeply unsettling depiction of man s comings-and-goings as an uncanny puppet show) as prime examples of horror feigned vs. horror embodied, Ligotti concludes that what makes man tragic is not simply that they are victims of nature, but that they are conscious of it both participants in and causalities of  numinous chaos at feast.

SOUL RETREIVAL
In soul retrieval, professional shaman/quack Villoldo proposes a four chambered model of the psyche as primordial Eden (chamber of wounds, of contracts, of grace, of treasure). These chambers map astonishingly well to the four-phrase mantra of Ho Opononpono: I am sorry (acknowledgement of mutual wounding), please forgive me (contractual erasure of wrongdoing), thank you (granting of grace), I love you (the largesse, the mother lode/lobe, the Treasure)

UNDISCOVERED SELF
Chapter 1   the plight of the individual in modern society
The plight of the man in modern society is active and invisible obstruction/suppression of self-knowledge (i.e., engagement in/with the irregularities of unmediated, individual experiences of the real) in favor of the massified ego-consciousness of the quasi-animate state that enforces statistical average through scientific rationality  
Chapter 2 - religion as the counter-balance to mass-mindedness
Distinguishing true religion from mere creed, Jung here points out the way in which the dictator states (and the credal religious institutions which subvert them in their insistence on credal conformity) usurps the God function, asserting the primacy of the statistical/scientific mode to the exclusion of any absolute reference point beyond the material/communal, thereby not only robbing man of his rights but also of his instinctive impulse toward the divine and the individuated experiences arising therefrom.
Chapter 3   the position of the west on the question of religion
In this chapter, Jung notes a  fatal parallelism  between the sterile moralities of the western Church-State and the collectivist materialism of the eastern Marxist dictatorships, insofar as both attempt to enforce a set of institutionally sanctioned belief and ideals, which in the case of the West represent a sad reduction/devaluation the riches of our inherited Judeo-Christian tradition with its emphasis upon the sanctity and dignity of the individual life. 
Chapter 4   man s understanding of himself
Positioning consciousness as that fundamental enigma, the precondition of being, which through constant friction against the body of unconscious memory and instinct generates dual and opposite impulses, Jung asserts that a balance must be struck between understanding of the subjectivities and knowledge of man s condition as a whole, in order that the individual man might reclaim the deep symbols of his deep past and integrate them in new and personalized ways, rather than sublimating them through mindless participation in the collective.
Chapter 5   philosophical and psychological approaches to life
In order to heal the disorder of the day (i.e., resolve the neurotic fractures that plague our modern consciousness), Jung asserts that we must undertake a systematic reinvigoration and reexamination of the life-giving symbols and primordial images that we ve inherited (conduits of dynamism and instinctive energies), taking care that we do not lose our shadow selves in collective ideals of statism and fetishization of the abstract Word.
Chapters 6 and 7   self-knowledge and the meaning of self-knowledge
In these final chapters, Jung further interrogates the obstacles which block man s achievement of self-knowledge (i.e. state-driven depotentiation/agglomeration of man as social unit and the isolation of his psychic energies), the means by which he may overcome these obstacles (i.e. personal acknowledgement of the dual nature of man, his shadow self, and the dynamic power of the psychological factor individual numinous encounter), and the importance of doing so (i.e. seizing this historical moment [Kairos   right time] to metamorphosize our tormented psyches, to deepen and broaden the consciousness, to unblock the dynamic flows of primitive  mana  that we might find the happiness and equability that only an individual self, never a state or collective body, can enjoy)
Further reflections
Ligotti and Jung both remark on the strange enigma of consciousness; however, for Ligotti a monistic materialist this puzzle cannot be addressed in any meaningful way as there is no one actually there to solve it (and thus nothing to solve), only agglomerations of matter which, through an unfortunate series of evolutionary/involutionary blunders, fancy themselves souls.
Jung, on the other hand, a committed dualist, is not so quick to dismiss parapsychological phenomena, instead maintaining the distinct possibility that there are psychic realities which supersede their physical manifestations.
In short, where Ligotti sees an illusion or delusion to be annihilated (objective scientific knowledge over subjective understanding), Jung sees a mythic adventure to be undertaken (individual irregularities brought to bear upon the statistical collective).

kygo - cloud nine
it takes you to a place that is at once safe and exotic; it's feels like a resort...the promise of possibility and the comfort of familiarity.....it's a kind of like, an affordable flight of fancy...it has the same sort of sunny, bourgeois sense of luxury and well being but also adventure that you get from like a cruiseline commerical.... it's like a vacation for the ears. i liked it. it's like stock music that you can sort of impose your own happy slideshow on top of. the rush of the unknown without any of the risk.

musically, it really is the apotheosis of the kind of electro indie pop that was really unique to the mid to erly 2010s ...which interestingly conincided with the birth and explosion in popularity of instagram (founded 2010). and I'm not making a causal assertion here, just a correlation I found interesting. because  really I think it's the same kind of impulse at work, this desire to sort of filter out experiential imperfections, to smooth them out, to color correct, to curate a sort of perpetual summer, where only good vibes are allowed. it's a false image, and a false sound...and we all know that, we all know that cloud nine can't bear the weight of human lives, but it's an ideal to strive toward, a shared ideal, and maybe the only shared ideal that we really have anymore as a society. but it also creates a kind of dissonance, which comes out in my favorite song from thw album, which is fiction--i cant take just a fictional you. we crave authentic connection, but to get it we'd have to sacrifice good vibes, so we end up caught between in this liminal daze.

This is probably neither the time nor the place nor the way to say this, but it needs to be said--forgive my artlessness.

Despite my best intentions, I am, I guess you could say, "catching feelings" that, given the circumstances, I ought not catch. 

The more time I spend with you, the more time I wish I were spending with you, in other ways, all ways.

I guess when I said in that panicked email awhile back that "I would love nothing more than to be friends" I was lying a bit, even if I didn't realize the extent of the lie at that time. Not to say I don't want friendship; I do, but there is something I would "love more," I'm realizing.

Now it most certainly was not a lie when I said that I would not and could not allow myself to jeopardize a long standing relationship. I respect you and Ryan way too much for that. So you don't have anything to worry about on that point. 

I think can push all of this aside once and for all--I know I can... these things pass with enough time and space--but before I let that happen, I felt I needed to make it explicit to you, just once. I hope it doesn't damage what we have or make things more complicated than they need to be, but I felt compelled to say it, against all my natural instincts.

So there you go. Do with it what you will.

I come time and again to this point, never beyond it: to fight or surrender. To fight is to pick a side, to stake yourself on a particularity. To surrender is to lay down the notion of sides, to resign yourself to the All-ness.

When I pick a side, I look around and see people dying, and quickly lose whatever taste for the fight I imagined I might have--return to neutral zone, crestfallen and humbled. 

When I surrender, I look around and see people living, and quickly lose whatever capacity for _detachment_ I imagined I might have--return to neutral zone, hot and lusty.

And when I attempt synthesis, that is to engage both impulses simultaneously in some way, to bloody the white flag or dress the red wound, to actively withdraw or to passively engage, I look around and cease to see people at all, just formless smears of half-ideated motion, a kind of mania or derealization--return to neutral zone, dazed and brimming. 

I need to be taken prisoner, conscripted, or else killed. But I cannot be left to my own devices much longer.

as a child, feeling safe was the sublime joy. a rainy day, the smell of something baking, the murmur of a television. Or the sun low in the sky on a drive back from some event or other, buckled up forehead against the window. or your mother putting her hand and to your head, "you're warm." or your father: "we'll figure it out."

what is comparable now, as an adult, grown beyond illusions of domestic safety, of the world as a kind and ordered place? a father God, a mother spirit? a home beyond the clouds? amplified projections of those early sense memories? likely.

but what of the troubled child, who has no such memory bed to rest upon, no loam of parental care from which to grow a spiritual sense? what of them? does not their plight cast doubt on the accidental nature of our optimisms, dependent as they are on experiences we just as easily may never have had?

undoubtedly. whether these doubts are well-founded is another question. institution says no. the zealous practitioner says no. all of our societal buttressing says, desperately, no, no--there is safety in the city, in the nation, in the bosom of progress. 

and yet by the very nature of these protestations against doubt--insistent, plaintive--Man betrays himself, reveals himself a child still, an aging vessel for the flame of nascence, reenactor of the fond recollection.

to those who lack the common ground, the conforming spirit, the stomach for such indulgent shadowplay in the light of some distant happiness--whether real or imagined, future or past--there is no alternative but a new worship, a black flame, a void instead of a God. 

to embrace the existential threat as one would a mother, to find safety in the absoluteness of death: this becomes the exile's creed. so does the occult cyst form below and beyond the town center. and so does the child become its own twin, one evil and one good, each orphaned from itself, lost and toddling about, weary as time, with no one to put him to bed.

like if close my eyes and dont really to the lyrics, i'm transported to like a dentist office, thats the closest thing I can compare it to
thid kind of sterile sleepy environement
i can almost hear the soft whirr of the drill and feel the anesthesia setting in
i suppose the dentist analogy woukdnt work for people who are scared of it, but the dentist never bothered me, it was always like a non-space, like that twilight state between awake and asleep--white walls, soft rock, old issues of highlights

for some reason thats where this album took me

and I know I'm sort of free associating here, but my mind was also taken to t.s. eliot's prufrock, specifically rhe line about the evening sky being spread out like a patient etherized on a table.

to ne, that great slumbering patient, in thecontext of this album, really both albums, is the modern christian institution, sort of motionless, inert, bereft of consciousness, in desperate need of like resuscitation really. just sort of drifting between worlds. praise and worship music is the soundtrack of that weird dream.

and the other thing that this sort of oblique appeal to modernist poetry brings out or brought to mind for me is the inward turn that you'vr seen in really in all art, both pop art and high art, since early twentieth century, and it trickled down even into the church. like I think every song on this album is written in first person, it's some speaker expressing how God makes them feel, usually through an appeal to these very tired old cliches about the spotless lamb or the cleansing river or the holy flame or whatever--but it's always in reference to the worshipper--I surrender, you fill me up...etc. spritiual feeling is what has precedence.

which contrastd sharply to the old traditional hymns or really in the old poetic mode, which tended to be second orthird person--they were less letters to God and more descriptions of God (a mighty fortress is our god, a bulwark never failing--today you are my mighty castle, you fill ne eith your strength). which i think is indicative of a lrager cultural shift, not necessarily a bad shift, but just a shift toward the self, where our objective beliefs in the metaphysical realities of the supernatural have been undermined by dcience and war and the advance of industry that the chruch and socirty more broadly was forced to reyreat to a place of subjective expression, ehich I think worship music gives voice to.

to be a person in the world
is a hard-to-swallow pill
to be a person that's a girl
is harder to swallow still

yet I've found there is a way
to make the pill go down
just take a sip of silence;
it's as easy as it sounds

turn off loud thoughts;
they only serve to parch and to constrict
and only you can counteract 
the pain that they inflict

and how? by simply taking hold 
of what is right before you
the cup of present happiness
will never not restore you

drink deeply of experience
aware and unattached
your self inflicted weariness
will turn to joy unmatched

forsake imagination 
of all past and future troubles
accept the gift of here and now
like children blowing bubbles

our feelings and our fantasies
will drift up and away
so take a sip of silence
and know it's all okay'

Text:
your honor may I approach the bench

you may

an old dog on a porch, looking up wearily

time settled around the courtroom, stray coughs.

your honor, the lawyer whispered, the evidence--it's admissible, all of it.

what evidence, said the judge, none has been presented.

exhibits A-D, they have strong bearing on my client's case, and attest inconvertibly to his innocence.

your honor, continued the lawyer, rubbing his chin, what would you say to this?

I will engage my opponent in a duel, ten paces, him to the [south] and myself to the [north]

go on, said the judge, pulling out a toothbrush out of robes.

if, said the lawyer, I maim but do not kill my opponent, you will acknowledge that (1) there is evidence and (2) it is admissible. If I kill my opponent, you will rule in the favor of my client, innocent of all charges.

you have no client, said the judge, spreading a thick blue smear of paste.

furthermore, continued the lawyer, if my opponent maims but does not kill me, then I will acknowledge that I have no evidence, no client, and that this is not a court of law. if he kills me, well, he will not kill me. I have it on heavenly authority.

the judge gingerly poured water from his glass onto the brush--too much, and it spilled onto his robes.

ah, bailiff, napkins please. the judge wiped at the wet spot irritably.

well, your honor?

do as you please, the judge snapped. I'm not a judge.

very well, said the lawyer. he walked briskly back to his seat, unclasped his gator skin portmanteau and withdrew a ornate flintlock pistol.

he began polishing the pewter piece as the jury looked on disinterestedly.

this went on for some time, and may very well still be.

there is no, can be no, sincerity in an environment where opinion is commodity and  engagement metrics (or buzz) are the prevailing social currency, backed only by empty techoinstitutional promises of "reach," inscribed as and subject to the fickle whims of algorithm. intent is an obsolete standard, nonfungible therefore inconsequential. even measures of approbation (likes/dislike) have less buying power than the gross sum of impressions. this is capital 3.0, erasure of the final vestige of the human interior.

what commonly passes for materialism is actually dualism reskinned, reinforcing the body-mind disjunction by positioning the "natural world" (a loaded and oft misapplied term) as separate from the humans that occupy it. the empirical stance is an inherently adversarial one. the new materialist school (ala Abram and his Husserlian ecophenomologist school) does more to reclaim our lost spiritual heritage than religious institutions while also leaving for scientific innovation/advancement, beckoning toward a new version of an old subject-object synthesis that neither the laboratory nor the chapel can sufficiently accomodate-- that neither the priest nor the scientist has yet to offer-- their respective dogmatics having led to imaginative bankruptcy and ontological isolation of the human from the more-than-human world

the basic precepts of buddhism seem to me to be present in just three of the many (many) intricately braided sets, series, groups, and progressions that comprise its fantastically systematic and numerological doctrines. they are: the four noble truths (life is suffering, suffering is desire, desire can be ended, the way to end it is the eightfold path), the eightfold path (wisdom group: right view, right intention; moral discipline group: right speech, right action, right livelihood; concentration group: right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration) and the defilements (greed, aversion, and delusion, with ignorance at the root)

the actual (and increasingly the stated) purpose of the education system is not to create curious well-informed individuals but a compliant and docile populace. america's rapid "decline" (relative to metrics that "matter") is a direct result of this, i think. collectivism has its merits, on the local level at least, but they become drawbacks at scale. the collective does not invent, it does not innovate, it does not drive growth. That's why communism always becomes authoritarianism; bereft of the engine of individuality, the inert state demands a strong and charismatic hand (which turns quickly to an iron fist). 
if you want to see the origins of the populist movement, look no further than the modern classroom (school as prison, students as inmates. their crime? having been born a self. "oh we will standardize that out of you in no time," say the well-intentioned educators, "we will make you an Average Citizen yet.")
look at their dead vacant eyes, sapped of vitality, dried-out tinder, awaiting the reactionary spark. the conflagration will spread (is spreading) fast and wide, carried on the winds of a nameless anger. 
like all civilizations, we have done this to ourselves, we have created the conditions for our own destruction. it could be no other way.

skirt coyly swishing
unattainable, a tan thumbnail of a vision

discernable even so far away as

of the external kind too

STYX
Out on the river Styx, explained our guide, careering the party along a red road that ran bumpily astride the thicketed bank, through which glimpses of the dark meandering body could here and there be caught, were any number of-- a devastating pothole clacked our teeth-- were any number of historical sights of import, and enjoyable activities for children or adults.

He pointed at a brochure taped overhead. The party shifted nervously, eyeing an oncoming bus, whose driver hailed ours--unheeded--then hurtled past with inches of clearance.  

"River Styx River Water Adventures." Unclear, I thought, how such a bad name could have come to be chosen.

We're nearly there, the driver drawled into the intercom. "We were nearly there 10 minutes ago," one of the party muttered. The others laughed gratefully for the attempt at levity. The driver glared in the rearview.

I turned to a woman with a towel around her, chewing gum, wearing cheap sunglasses with the tag still on. "You believe this?" I asked.

"Oh yeah. You've never been?"

"On the river Styx? No."

"Ah, well it's a fun time."

I looked out at the hot gray sky. "Maybe we'll get some sun finally," I offered.

"Maybe," she declined.

I'd been recently given a coupon for a "tubing experience" on this river I'd never heard of an hour's drive across the state line and--having recently entered one of my not infrequent periods of performative crisis--I decided what the hell. 

A fly in a long-abandoned web--forehead against glass, the metaphor came to me--is what I was. Mine, I knew, was to be a slow, meaningless passing, without even the satisfaction or life-affirming indignity of being a meal for some sultry many-eyed thing who might afterwards call herself my widow. No, impotent and alone, I would wriggle halfheartedly once or twice a year, imagine myself capable of something grand and significant, or at the very least something new, before once again gassing out, quieting down, remembering myself as I was and had been for some two decades now, ensconced in my silken complacency, the secretive little sufferings and comforts of a bachelor and a reasonably happy man. 

The bus squealed. Aaaand everybody out. Out we tumbled, coolers and bags and accouterments in tow. We were instructed to select a "tubular flotation dee-vice" from an unhitched trailer bed on which they were piled. A din of squeaking rubber. Exposed pillbugs rolled and scattered damply. "Look mom," some little bowl-cut terror had climbed up and was stomping them. "Bugs."  

The mother, digging sand from a small clenched mouth, grunted dispassionately. "Down."

Dragging our tubes to the river's edge, there was a grimness in the air, something imminent. The last portage of a fated platoon, death in their eyes. I could see it, the haunting footage of roughnecked men in flip flops and their trailer trash consorts, time-flecked, hollowed-out faces, slow pans set to somber fiddles and the rich intonation of MALE VOICE from every slapped together war-fetish "back then" slop-umentary you've ever seen. Pop. I jumped at a not-so-distant gunshot, sudden diegesis.

"Gun range down the way," said the guide, chuckling past me. He tossed his tube out and leapt onto it, bobbing down and away.

Chastened, I gingerly followed, lowering myself in, hissing as the frigid water touched my ass. God. I read somewhere, a study, that quiet people suffer more to cold and heat, feel more. I took comfort in that.

There was the bowl-cut kid, utterly unaffected by it, splashing in the murk and dunking his brother. "Stop. Hey." Mother already had a beer in hand, watching the Father(?) try to wedge a cooler in a tube. 

Beyond them, I saw the woman in the cheap shades, applying sunscreen on the bank, working it into herself, kneading curves. Should I...? A wave, a beckoning gesture?

But the current had already caught me and I was off, the ones ahead pulling away fast and the ones behind growing small.

I floated for awhile, cold and regretful, missing my chair and my book, earmarked where I'd left it on the sidetable. Another gunshot rang out, closer. 

What would MALE VOICE say of this--two gunshots now--of the lone recruit, deep in unfamiliar territory, adrift on death's own river, "'twixt shrouded banks, bristling and scornful?" Well, what could be said? There was no wartime correspondence, no body of work, no letters to mother or a cherry-lipped beloved--no surviving comrade to inflate with teary testimony my final heroisms. What could be said of him then, of this hapless man floating here, lacking both a battle to fight and a means to fight it?

"'Twixt shrouded banks," I repeated uneasily, making out a sign posted ahead; nailed askew to a fallen tree, it punctuated my vague run-on ruminations, gave form to them, a reptilian one, silhouetted against caution yellow. 

Arching my back, I dabbed nervously, then desperately against the water's pull, willing myself back to center, away from the shaggy overhang, and the things that drop from them, or lurk beneath them--tensed ancient jaws, eyes filmy with instinct.

Overcompensating, I erred toward the opposite bank, avoided it barely too, then back across again--terror, a minute's relief, terror--and in this zig-zag manner I made my way down a fair stretch until at last--wincing, the scratch and crackle of branches--a too-sharp bend left me clinging to a slimy trunk, sucked tight against it. 

Calming somewhat, embarrassed somewhat, I spotted a stick, sensed its potential. Yanking it loose from the snarl, I used it to push free--proud of myself, hesitantly.  

With my makeshift oar, I was able to scull a straight course, growing in confidence enough for my mind to wander back to the woman in shades, inventing unlikely occasions for chivalry: she was drowning, she was stuck, her top had come undone and I retrieved it, eyes gallantly averted. Ridiculous, these fantasies. Did other people have them? Of course. That was the benefit of reading, to be reassured that your sad little dreams were not in fact your own, but the shared property of Man. They gave testament, our books and stories, to the universality of the strange and fragile experience. 

And how fragile, I thought, following the thick oily artery of the river, down, down, past the vignettes of decay and oldblood Carnival on one side and the other: trailers on stilts, sagging and green; scum-lined dinghies docked in stagnant little inlets where cans and styrofoam stippled the shallows; past sandbar revelries, redneck Bacchus cavorting in the company of his Dixieland maenads; past the dark mouths of storm drains--or what I hoped were storm drains--emitting a sulfuric reek.

It went on like this, like a series of stills in rapid succession--time slaking, sloughing itself, bunching up in a whirring projective motion, spooled along this brimstone backwater under sepia skies, wet and mottled, montage disjunction...

[MALE VOICE]

I saw a totem pole strung with telephone lines, flying the Stainless Banner. 

I saw a lawn jockey in a tree stand, defaced like an old god. 

I saw Robert E. Lee in a mobility scooter, cursing, ensnared in kudzu.

I saw, I see myself in ragged grays, a breechloader on my shoulder, hunched and creeping at the flanks--steady men--a sharp flicking past the ears, ffttt, thudding dully an inch off the mark. Blankness, euphoria, then the sick churning of realization, a backward misstep on the scree, tumbling, scrambling, mad animal flight. Ffftt. fffttt. Flickering fast now, silver halide washing out red, the chemical wetness, burnthrough.... a bugle call, distorted, reverberant, then the roar of engagement; men hot for death, marked for it. 

----

A dragonfly landed on my knee, tickling return to present. I smacked at it, looked around. The sun was sinking low; my tube was too, a slow leak from the snag no doubt.

Coming round yet another bend, in the gloaming, I saw the shape of two men in lawn chairs on a point bar, a small lantern at their feet. I clambered out and over to them, my feet sinking deep in the slick pluff of the riverbed. The tube trailed behind me like a limp skin.

"Heya," said one of the men around his dangling cigarette. Approaching him, his [cheekbones] deepened in the lantern light, skeletal. 

"Hey. I'm--" What to even say. "You know where the return point is? For, the river tour. Adventure." 

The man shrugged. "Man, I guess it's a mile back. Or a mile up. You want a cig."

I took one, consigned to the weirdness of it. The lighter flicked.

"So," I took a drag, coughed. Frogs were croaking loudly from the wings in tenuous unison. We watched silently as the last red wound on the western sky congealed and blackened. 

"It's like with Zeus and them." The man said it with authority. His friend remained mute.

"It's like what?" I hadn't smoked in years; it hit me, vertiginous. 

"Like with Zeus and them." He gestured at the river, black and chuckling. "The myths."

Smoke wreathed his face. I groped for something, anything to hang this moment on, a reference point, a precedent. The fissure widened below--yawning, sheer--an infinite strangeness, things slipping away from themselves, caught between banks a universe across, mirrored doubly, trebly, a thousand times over, then shattered at last on the punctum of the bugle's sanguine call.

"I guess it is," I finally said.

I heard 
the mother's reville chirping
  from my nested-in eaves
to rouse her eggy young
 out the gnawed through buttonhole
into bright June

threaded along deftly
on a blue strung needle
she sewed to the sky around them 
a breastpocket
of eagerness and care
"see! see! the world!"

ungainly, they fluttered forth 
on downward courses
new to the pull
of wind 
and grass 
and the glinting water's
surface

that gives and swallows,
a chlorinated lethe

it was unlucky variance for one
that took its errant flight path 
to the hot white pool deck 
ill fated

and 
from my porch I watched it,
quizzical,
hop lightly in
(no diving!)
and learn the only thing 
it ever would

about thrust and drag and lift
which it couldnt get 
with its wet wings 
and soft bones

drawing pitiful cursive
on the deep end substrate
lacework ripples
on that lazy blue square 
that no one saw but me
and the recourseless mother
"see! see!"

dashing down,
i hurdled the locked gate
(no running!)
and scooped it up 

too late, 
filmy-eyed
death-spraddled
beak agape and dribbling

it vacated its bowels in my hands
and stiffened

later, inside
hands washed
I chanced to see the mother on the railing
looking in at me

whose cherubic features do to the eye suggest
two rising suns, bathing east and west

in rays more luminous than all the hosts of heav'n
to my fallen spirits, she like leaven

doth this doughy member bring to springing back to life
ella, my love, my heart, my wife

twere i but a solitary meteor, and she the fertile sphere 
gladly would i plummet and burn up in her atmosphere

were she the ocean, and i one single grain of sand
id settle in her trackless depths, and irritate her clam

Till from it, lustrous pearls emerge, her shapely bosom to adorn
she the unblemished rose, and i her guardian thorn

in the trees i see her, a syvlan maiden fair
in the moon, the sky, the waves.. I see her also there

and in the quietude of humid vernal nights
i think of her, and all is set to rights

Bereft by love, impaled on cupid's dart
ella, to you i give my soul, my life, my heart

i could quit my job. I am not in any way obligated to this company. it is a business exchange: labor for compensation. they do not "deserve" my loyalty, my time, or my effort beyond that which is contractually obligated. from a market perspective, I am underpaid, given my experience and education. i will work hard, of course, i owe it myself to do so. i do not owe it to them. i am the one in control here. they need my labor more than i need their money. i could get money elsewhere. in fact, i may do so. i am not yet sure what money making venture would suit me better. something creative i think. but for now, my goal is to help them get a system off the ground, a database specifically. they may not be on board with that. 

i think i need to take time off and go to colorado. i need to think about where I'm at. 

i also want to go to europe i guess. gay paris.

i undestand where they are coming from. I am not frustrated with myself or with them. i am committed to making the most out of this time at cvp, however long it may be, andl learning to strike a better balancr between receptive and active. i want to get things done, not piddly little pat on the back things, real things.

she moved thru the underbrush
big around as a tyre
with a beady eyed desire
and a cold knowing
old as the meandering river
sleek and glistening with meals

the metal man through beeping one day
on his clumsy wheels
his mouth was a speaker behind a grill
playing chiptunes

bodhis sat vascillating 
on the stoop
thinking of famine, war 
and croup

these things were not good
they were seemingly bad
he belched and smelled
the meal he just had

a parade was ongoing
projectile vomit
and beads 
and jasmine was growing;
a bum was relieving
himself on its leaves

there were fat men wearing pirate hats
and fat women who appeared to be liking that
and children in strollers
and jackbooted patrollers
and bells and bell tollers
and all the kings horses and all of his men

all of whom bodhis roundly condemned 
there from his place on the stoop

where he sat thinking of war and of croup 
and the crap
that the bum now was doubtlessly taking 
the mess he was sute to be making
on flowers
 untrimmed and spreading
from alleyways narrow as shoulders
of priests

the sky was shedding its
daylight, picking its molars
and smiling its creased and 
 gummy red smile
bodhis reviled this
pursing his lips against its lewd advances

while across the way,
gently the Virgin 
blue in flickering light
watched courtyard romances

two lovers spilling their beers
their words their seed

She placidly looked on 
in spite of it
just to the right of it
beheld in between and beyond 
what Bodhis could not

squinting and rubbing his belly
in fear of heeding the dyspeptic thought

that in all this grand contumely
the many costumed procession
of man and his making

there was no heart worth having or breaking
no cause for confessions
or cause for aught at all

bodhis thought of this and more 
from his seat on the stoop
in spring's soft pall

while the bum ambled on 
looking for
quieter places
to answer natures call

and children in strollers
and jackbooted patrollers
and bells and bell tollers
and all the kings horses and all of his men

all of whom bodhis roundly condemned 
there from his place on the stoop

where he sat thinking of war and of croup 
and the crap
that the bum now was doubtlessly taking 
the mess he was sute to be making
on flowers
 untrimmed and spreading
from alleyways narrow as shoulders
of priests

the sky was shedding its
daylight, picking its molars
and smiling its creased and 
 gummy red smile
bodhis reviled this
pursing his lips against its lewd advances

while across the way,
gently the Virgin 
blue in flickering light
watched courtyard romances

two lovers spilling their beers
their words their seed

She placidly looked on 
in spite of it
just to the right of it
beheld in between and beyond 
what Bodhis could not

squinting and rubbing his belly
in fear of heeding the dyspeptic thought

that in all this grand contumely
the many costumed procession
of man and his making

there was no heart worth having or breaking
no cause for confessions
or cause for aught at all

bodhis thought of this and more 
from his seat on the stoop
in spring's soft pall

while the bum ambled on 
looking for
quieter places
to answer natures call

Text:
ATHG works, against all odds, because it commits utterly to itself...no fourth wall breaking, no winking at the audience, no lazy non sequiturs. 

it doesnt feel like something that was made or written so much as discovered. an alien organic thing.

its nonsense, but internally cohesive nonsense that rings true in a fever dream sort of way. it has a complete confidence in itself, which comes i think from being truly the first thing of its kind, immaculately conceived. it doesnt have to "restrain" or "punch up".. . because its not a fiction. its an accurate depiction of an unhinged vision, a very real thing.

JERRY SPRINGER

a sensational integrative force. a pillar of sanity and showmanship and good humor, shedding for us a wry, clear light on suppressed and subaltern (un)realities of his time and all time. 
profanities, grotesqueries, perversions: these were his trade and he plied it well, eyebrow permanently raised (and what do you think of THIS dear viewer?), tongue planted firmly in the cheek of a dawning millennium
note if you will how effortlessly he works the room, now from the audience, now from the stage--negotiating, initiating, deescalating--sprezzatura itself--toeing with unflappable poise the line between entertainment and exploitation, between theater and therapy, between man and his shadow

Text:
SEVERANCE
The man at the bar was reasonably certain his dog was trying to communicate with him, and had been for some time. 
"I saw a look in his eye that was human." 
Severance looked over at the man at the bar and shook his head. "That can't be true." 
"He arranges objects in uncanny ways. Errant toys and bones and household items."
"I don't think that's possible." 
"He makes water discreetly and at regular intervals."
"I don't think that's possible." Severance signalled to the bartender. "What's it got to with anything? Passing water."
"He passes water on the hour. It's uncanny."
"That's nonsense. Another beer."
"Come over on Sunday night and I'll show you and you can be the judge."
Severance shrugged and drank in silence. He and the man both stood up at the same time to leave.
***
Sunday night, Severance came around with a bottle of wine. The dog was sleeping on a chair, raised an eye at him. 
"Well I'm here." 
"Yep. This is the dog. Here boy." 
The dog climbed stiffly down with the air of an alderman went to the bookshelf and nosed a volume.
"See that? He's aware and capable." 
 Never mind that.  
 See? These are the archives.  The man thumbed several pages in.  April 15, a message was recieved. Bones of a bird, unmistakably cruciform. Intentional. 
Severance looked at the dog and then the man.  That s nothing.  
 Listen. April 18, another message in bones. 3 lizards, their skeletons carefully articulated. 
 Mm. Where should I put this wine.  Severance went to the kitchen and began rummaging through the empty cabinets.
 Anywhere. Listen. May 1, discovery of an old shed north of the creek. Dilapidated.  The dog began to whine and pace.  Contents included nests, bones. Smell of piss and shit.  
Severance came back with mason jars to pour in.  You never told me you had land. 
 Family land, twenty acres of it. Thank you.  The man sipped.  What kind of name is Severance anyway. 
 Never mind that. Take me to this shed. 
***
It was 9 o clock when they reached it, holding lanterns and high-stepping through the knee-deep kudzu.
 See? That s the place. Here boy.  
The dog barked furiously and ran inside. The men followed to see it pissing in a corner, staring. 
  Left corner, unusual. That could be indicative.  The man took out his notebook.
 You re making something of nothing. You re imposing meaning.  Severance knelt down, picked up an old bottlecap. On the underside, it read,  You have won! 
Like an angel from god, the dog leapt out at him; lantern shattered and the place went up in flames. It took 10 days and crews from three counties to put it out.

The drumsticks of war 
Beat darkly in the breast
Of the quick brown fox
Coxcombing through his texts
For native notions to behead
Poultry in motion e'er the dead
ones, the fried-in-bread ones
Could raise their ancient cry
Musket powder ink blot
eggfaced crackpots
Pen the henhouse red and
Smoke the peace pipe dry

No fear, 
Chanticleer
for John Smith farms organically
The colors of the wind will be harnessed quite sustainably

And for your bravery
a feathered cap and gown
A thornless crown, 
no cross left to bear
or knee to wound
Or squaws to squabble or prepare your wretched food 

No brooks left to ford
Or arguments left to brook,
Chief among them, Lord, "to take back what was took"
to unbreak that omlette 
unkill mohamet
Uneat the sour grapes
of our hegemonic rapes 
and pillages
(sorry about your villages)

but that's life
And the chicken chicks on chippily
"bookbook"ing while the foxes
Look on licking lippily

ive been puzzling for a long time over why transsexuality has become such a pervasive issue over the past decade and why it's defended with increasingly religious fervor.

it struck me today that maybe it is because the concept of transexuality fills a Christ vacuum in the imagination of a recently secularized public. 

the transexual could be seen as a new christ, the integrative third that disrupts the old binary. paradox incarnate, reassigned, reborn, renamed, reviled...the archetypal victim, with surgical wounds for stigmata. phallic cross and yonic tomb. miraculous offspring of Father-God Science and Mother-Spirit Sex. 

I just can't help but think that this whole marketplace of late stage capital where sexual identity is bought and sold, is on temple grounds, so to speak (or the ruins of those grounds)...trading on the "symbolic salvage" of the gutted Judeo-Christian framework. That people feel the loss of that framework whether they know it or not. And that LGBTQ+, and particularly the trans figure, has the kind of cultural sway it does because of its messianic glimmer, delivery for the oppressed, satisfying in a certain way the need for some deeply held contradiction, without which there is no foundation for any justice at all

lesson 9:
depart, you narrow minded hens
you dealers of inconsequence
get thee hence, dawdlers and dilettantes
and trouble me no more

I'd rather breathe the musty spores
from pages of old books
then smell the smell of flapping lips
and dodge your darting looks
I'd rather feel the wind
or its calm absence
than touch your heated skin
and bear that sentence
that crude brand upon my forehead 
where you kissed me
marred me.

"did you miss me?"

miss? I'd rather die
then miss or be missed by you
I've seen your wares
your wheatless tares
and have no wish to try you

depart! begone!
and leave me to my leavings
infant-like
you gnaw to soothe your carnal teethings
diapered, powdered
toddling yon and thither
or else, cold-eyed and serpentine
you slither

depart
depart, I'd rather die
than listen to your prattling
one moment more;
than be the man stood hat in hand
at your hell-darkened door.
(all the sweaty nothings
traded so cheaply in that place - 
chimeric, emetic, disgrace upon disgrace)

I'd rather die, yes, die,
than be the man who felt you 
and was felt.
I'd rather die
than be the man who smelt you 
and was smelt.

RIVERGIRL
they found her in the bulrushes 
crawling around
a reedy little thing
with sly eyes and sharp teeth
and the sun in her belly

"my daughter of rivers" cooed the barren queen
and stroked her brown cheek 
her tangled hair 
which soon would be shaved in the style of the times,
face painted finely
(all to her great discomfiture)

that was how the river girl
assumed unlikely form 
as terror of the palace;
her list of sins grew long.
many a beleagured tutor or suitor
lost his wig and sandals.
she bit a maid's finger,
defaced the grounds,
pissed once in an ointment pot.

oh but punishments were paltry.
remonstrances, a giggling matter
her eyes played guessing games
feral glints of something hidden, something known,
half-answers to old riddles

her majesty would not feign 
an absent air from royal litters,
but threw her jewelry at the crowds 
with epithets of glee

she portended ill, priests murmured,
cause of a bad harvest, or a plague

but the moon knew otherwise.
waxing full, it watched her 
steal long walks beyond the walls,
pious in the soft night, 
barefoot through riverbeds, 
rich mud clinging 
like a child to a mother

and the crocodiles croaked their prophecies
and the holy bullfrogs chanted

overcome with sanctity,
she whispered prayers to gods that were not hers
halting supplications
arbitrary penances:
	cuts with stones, bites from asps, 
	lying naked on the sharp-hewn stubble

purified in this way, she learned to fly 
with cranes and ibises 
high above snaking sands
to scout entrances to tombs

so that by torchlight, descending
hunched through halls and long-sealed chambers
she might bless them,
the desiccated dead

and so in the night, in the early dawning hours,
the moon watched the rivergirl drift away
knowing the sadness of wisdom and
the coldness of time

Text:
life is a little idea that climbed into the ear of God. it attached itself and whispered dreams that sproted and budded, climbed out in and around god's head in a great profusion of curling fingers. life covered his arms and legs, filled his insides and found his orifices. a verdant madness, bacchic, tumbling and twining themselves together these many vines formed striated bands which formed limbs, and a trunk, a great green thoughtless body, with the sleeping body of god at with heart. the little idea in the ear of god in the heart of the great green one rejoiced at this and capered and sang a many pitched song, which the  green body danced to, whirling itself through dark reaches and flinging spores, which found their way to other gods, and the lost children of those gods, prodigal and  sleeping in distant places, the pockets of void. Life went there, and seeded them with dreams, filling up the nothing with the tickling growth and leafy density, growing and growing, till every square inch of the vastness infinity was heavy with life and nothing was no more.
life fell on its small back and sighed with relief. yawning, it slept and dreamed an older dream, large and austere...of flint, and hammers, and sparks of a coming cleansing fire.
poem:
life is a little idea that climbed into the ear of God. 
it attached itself and whispered dreams that sproted and budded,
climbed out in and around god's head 
in a great profusion of curling fingers. 
life covered his arms and legs, 
filled his insides and found his orifices. 
a verdant madness, bacchic, 
tumbling and twining themselves together 
these many vines formed striated bands which formed limbs, 
and a trunk, a great green thoughtless body, 
with the sleeping body of god at with heart. 

the little idea in the ear of god 
in the heart of the great green one 
rejoiced at this and capered 
and sang a many pitched song, which the  green body danced to, 
whirling itself through dark reaches and flinging spores, 
which found their way to other gods, 
and the lost children of those gods, prodigal and  sleeping 
in distant places, the pockets of void. 

Life went there, and seeded them with dreams, 
filling up the nothing with the tickling growth 
and leafy density, 
growing and growing, 
till every square inch of the vastness of infinity 
was heavy with life and nothing was no more.

life fell on its small back and sighed with relief. 
yawning, 
it slept and dreamed an older dream, 
large and austere,
of flint, and hammers, 
and sparks of a coming cleansing fire.

STAV
i dont begrudge stav his newfound niche. i find him increasingly annoying and hacky, but here are few well known comedians who dont fit that description. the fact is, to acheive mainstream success, you need a clearly defined image: the simpler and stupider the better. stav's image is fat horny guy--played out maybe--but he certainly fits the part. i honestly dont think hes working under delusions of grandeur or genius--he just wants to be a household name, so hes leaning into the one angle that could feasibly get him there. tldr; you cant hold people to standards that exceed their ambitions.

randomness within set bounds, i.e. the d-20, the shuffled deck of cards. games of "chance" (ludic, atomic, cosmic)

but is the random element actual or merely perceptual? take the die. the outcome of a cast seems random to the casual observer, but in fact is dictated by physical and compositional variables (angle and speed of cast, the material of the die itself, texture of the casting surface, etc.)
couldnt the same be true of the electron and its seemingly arbitrary movements at the quantun level? is indeterminacy simply a determination deferred or missed? it would seem so. I suspect it is not the objects that are uncertain, but subjective attempts to model them

a can, warped by the heat of cigarettes dropped in it
the night whistles

norman rockwell painted warm lights
he sits in the mist in February
limning a gospel 
that does not include me

bare trees ache, their nerves exposed to
all of this and more
my twiggy starling
if you can walk around the place enough
the skin will grow over
and the hair will grow on it

and the wind will comb it out
the nits and nests 
into the pool to sink down sodden 
and clog up the drain,
lost continents

for a boy or girl to find, 
inhabited by the ghost of magellan or
the ghost of desoto 

those explorers in hats who
sat a long time in the rusted out deck chairs
waiting for summer
to watch wasps
zig zag 
toward a spot on a map only they can see

I cannot see it now
in the mists of february 
on my balcony overlooking 
all of this and more 

I cannot see it now 
but on my third or fourth time around
walking again past that grimacing tree
or that empty pool
in the mist
in february

I think I can see it

and how it beckons
and how and how

NABE
Mother's carpet matched her drapes
she built up static on them
produced a field for grazing cows
to wander and to low in

polar bears shorn close beseech the farmer
for his subsidies
his fallow widow lactates syrup
for their bear necessities

ground control to ursa major,
Tom's a minor inconvenience
divining futures in the pastures
show the rod and spare no lenience 

said the king of Babylon, his whoring 
wife Delilah with him
darning socks of Samsonsite [with Samson' hair]
for fetishists to wear and sniff em

poring over manuscripts and marginalia?
stick with dynamite and it'll never fail ya

Tom's cooking up anarchic schemes and dreaming of a better linen
to drape across the nakedness of all his father's 
mother's children

conical her titties, long her dugs,
she never should have given Aldous drugs

drugs so bravely manufactured by 
machine-predicted laborers
whose future selves earn salaries
to free their indebted embryos

womb suspension sweatshop amniotics
wrung from all of those
who have the sense to sense the corpse
that's hanging from a garden hose

in deep Edenic ungroomed thickets 
pubis of the farmer's wife
who levels barrels at the boy
for monkeying with death and life

and fancying a comsonaut who barked his shins
and gnashed his teeth on bones and made a crown of them

there's Uncle, he walks on all fours
driven from his habitat
penis long as yarrow stalks
and scarely quite as thick as that

the King now shows in hand in mortal sympathy
he'd never knowed or crowed a cock as limp as thee

I, Ching Chong, the chinaman
beheld the calving bergs unfurling
brackish bricks of soda pop
(no zone left) and cameras rolling

filmy substance, oil slick
atop the poor boy's Coca Cola
Tommy asked the who man 
could he maybe borrow his Corolla

but conversion's still forthcoming
catalytic proselyte
your ruddy hooded ornament
shall not adorn my sleigh tonight

and that's when Tom exhausted pinched the candle light

and so the father and the son
side by side embosom-ed
push up daisies and brasseries
hereafter quite un-cozen-ed

WOODS*
There once lived a boy with his mother and his father in a house at the edge of the woods. 
The boy would play in the grass and the in stream that flowed through the grass. But he never went into the woods. 
For every night, as they sat by the fire, his mother would tell him stories about the things that lived there, deep in the trees.  Children get lost in those woods,  she would say,  and they never come back again.  
His father would smoke his pipe and stare at the fire and say nothing.  
On cool spring days when the sun was high, he would  the trees would wave their branches at the boy, and the sparrows would sing to him  come along, don t be afraid, come along.  
But he heeded his mother's words and kept near the house, where he was safe.
On warm summer nights, he would look out his window, into the silver moonlight, and the slender beeches would dance for him. The bullfrogs would croak,  join us, join us, do not sleep.  
But the boy remembered what his mother said and pulled the covers over his head, dreaming of the woods and the things that lived there. 
Then one September day, his mother fell ill. She tossed and she turned, growing pale and thin. The boy's father sat by her bed day and night, smoking his pipe and saying nothing. The boy stared sadly out at the trees, which no longer waved or danced, but only swayed mournfully and wept their autumn leaves.
A week passed. The sickness got no better. At last, his father, looking grim, took the pipe from his mouth and said,  The time has come, boy. The time has come for you to go into the woods. You must find the plant with the purple flowers that will make your mother well.  
 But children get lost in the deep, dark woods and never come back again,  said the boy, frightened.  
 You must go all the same. Here, take this.  
His father handed him an satchel and a knife.  One to put things in, the other to take things out,  he said. Then with a pat on the head and a sad sad smile, his father sent him on his way.
As he came to the edge of the woods, the boy looked up at the trees. They did not seem to dance or wave or even sway mournfully. They bristled their beards at him and raised their great branches like claws. Even the ravens cried down at him from the branches,  no further, little boy, no further. 
But the boy had to go further, all the same.
Clutching the old satchel his father gave him, the boy took a deep breath and a big step. Then another and another and another. until turning around he could no longer see the little house at the edge of woods where his father smoked his pipe and his mother lay ill in her bed.

He was alone in the deep, dark forest where little boys get lost and never come back again.
 I have to find the purple flower,  the boy whispered to himself.  I have to make my mother well.  
 Purple flower?  came a voice.  Mother? 

The boy looked and saw it was a talking tree.
 
 I am lost,  the boy cried. 
 Lost?  asked the tree.  But the ground is right beneath you, and the sky is right above. How can you be lost?  
 I have to find the purple flower that will make my mother well, then bring it back to the little house at the edge of the woods.  
 No wonder you are lost,  the tree laughed.  You do not know where you are going. 
 I just told you,  cried the boy.  I am going to find the flower. 
 There is no purple flower,  said the tree.  Or at least, not in these woods.  
 Where then?  asked the boy, desperately.
 In the woods within the woods. The deepest, darkest place.  said the tree. And with that he went to sleep.

QUANTA - the discrete transmission 
QUALIA - the received characteristics

the semiotic configuration of plato's cave: the source, the surface, the thing (absence and presence), the man (author and reader/priest and prisoner)

the source is excitation of universal functions or loops, pure quanta.

the surface is known boundary against which source is registered.

the thing is manipulable reference point, projected as absence onto surface by source. 

the man is a source-thing, capable both of manipulating and being manipulated. as reader, he beholds; as author, he beholds beholding.

source enables, thing blocks, surface receives, man processes. 

surface < thing < source 

the eye is surface. all things are beheld upon surface by way of source. what the thing is is not known. what the source is is not known. only that it is felt is known. and that knowing becomes truer the fewer surfaces there are mediating the perceptive process.
